elements.sent-2411-051: We now describe the oracle problem we use for the conditional $\QMA$ vs. $\QCMA$ separation.
elements.sent-2411-052: Informally, the oracle is promised to encode an exponentially-large constant-degree graph that has either (at least) two connected components, or is a connected expander graph; the task is to distinguish between the two cases.

elements.sent-2411-054: First, we specify the oracle model.
elements.sent-2411-057: The oracles we consider are functions $F: [R] \times [N] \to [N]$ such that for all $r \in [R]$, $F(r,\cdot)$ describes a perfect matching on $[N]$: $F(r,x) = y$ if and only if $F(r,y) = x$.
elements.sent-2411-058: We call such a function $F$ a graph oracle because it naturally corresponds to a (multi)graph $G_F$ with vertex set $[N]$ and there is an edge $(x,y)$ if there exists an $r$ such that $F(r,x) = y$.
elements.sent-2411-059: In fact, the graph oracle $F$ encodes more information in that each edge $(x,y)$ has a color corresponding to the variable $r$ such that $F(r,x) = y$.
elements.sent-2411-060: A quantum query algorithm can query a graph oracle $F$ via the unitary $U_F : \ket{r,x,u \mapsto \ket{r,x,u \oplus F_r(x)}$ where $\oplus$ denotes addition over the binary field (we identify $[N]$ with binary strings $\{0,1\}^n$).}

elements.sent-2411-063: Now we formally define the oracle problem, which we call $\Components$.

elements.sent-2411-065: The $\Components$ problem is to distinguish whether a given graph oracle $F$ is in $\Lyes$ or $\Lno$, promised that one is that case.
elements.sent-2411-066: Natarajan and Nirkhe~\cite{natarajan2023distribution show that the $\Components$ can be solved in the $\QMA$ model: given oracle access to an instance $F \in \Lyes$, the prover can send the verifier the following $\log N$-qubit state: \begin{equation}\ket{\psi} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}}\sum_{x \in S} \ket{x} - \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}} \sum_{x \notin S} \ket{x}~.\end{equation}.}

elements.subsection-2411-004: An unconditional $\QMA$ vs. $\QCMA$ separation for interactive games and distributional oracles

elements.section-2411-007: Unconditional $\QMA$ vs $\QCMA$ separation for an interactive game
elements.sent-2411-610: In this section, we give an unconditional $\QMA$/$\QCMA$ oracle separation for an interactive oracle distinguishing game.
elements.sent-2411-611: This result leads to a simplified proof of the $\QMA$ and $\QCMA$ separation relative to a distributional oracle.
elements.sent-2411-612: The techniques we use to analyze the lower bound for interactive games have appeared in the literature for dealing with non-uniformity of algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-613: These are algorithms that have an unbounded preprocessing stage to produce an advice (which corresponds to the witness in the language of QMA/QCMA) that helps a bounded second stage algorithm to solve a challenge.
elements.sent-2411-614: The formulation of interactive games provides an alternative and clean view to the distributional oracle separation in previous works, demonstrating their conceptual similarity.
elements.sent-2411-615: The oracle has a similar structure to the one used in \cite{natarajan2023distribution and our previous sections, but with a few modifications.}
elements.sent-2411-616: The proof roadmap is as follows: we first define an interactive variant of the $\QMA$/$\QCMA$ protocol, and give an oracle separation for this variant through a technique that removes the use of witness in our analysis with a sequentially repeated game (multi-instance security).
elements.sent-2411-617: Finally, we show a reduction from the oracular interactive $\QMA$/$\QCMA$ game to a distributional oracle $\QMA$/$\QCMA$ game.
